notes.note: Cicero is at work on his philosophical project of 45 BC (the Academica and De Finibus), arguing against the Epicurean school's doctrine that pleasure is the highest good. The pre-dawn labour by lamplight is a recurring image of his furious productivity that summer.

Cicero's nephew, young Quintus Cicero, son of his brother Quintus and of Pomponia (Atticus's sister); the wayward youth whose double-dealing toward his estranged parents is the subject of letters 13.38--13.41.

notes.anchor: our Brutus's daily and tireless praise
notes.note: M. Iunius Brutus, whose repeated praise of Cicero has, Cicero supposes, finally shamed the young Quintus into writing him a civil letter.

Cicero quotes the Greek lyric poet Pindar (fr. 213 Snell) on the dilemma of whether to speak the plain truth or take "crooked" paths; the line frames his question of how to handle his treacherous nephew.
Cicero's favourite country house at Tusculum in the Alban hills southeast of Rome, his main retreat for writing during this period.

A small island-villa at the mouth of the Astura river on the Latian coast, where Cicero often went to be alone, especially after his daughter Tullia's death; he weighs it as a place to avoid an awkward meeting.
